title: Countryside worker
change: Occupational standard and assessment plan revised
overviewOfRole: Carry out specific environmental and conservation tasks.

knowledges.detail: How the different types of boundaries, including hedges, fences, walls, relate to their location and usage.
How the management of boundaries varies depending on their function and on local and regional factors for example geology, soil type, climate change and traditional style.
The legal status of access, including Public Rights of Way and land designated as Open Access.
The different types of path surfaces and structures and the suitability of these for different areas and usage.
How to identify British native and non-native flora and fauna including invasive plants, their lifecycle and how they are considered during habitat management work.
Techniques used to carry out surveys and how they are used to inform decisions on habitat, species, or access management in a specific location.
Habitat and vegetation management techniques, how their suitability varies depending on the locality and how they are used to promote conservation.
British standards that apply to constructing site furniture locally and how appropriate adaptations might be made depending on the topography, the geographical location and local style.
How to construct or maintain site furniture using sustainable materials where possible.
How to maintain and use hand tools and powered tools for example chainsaws and brushcutters.
How to safely handle and apply pesticides and the alternative methods for pest control that minimise environmental impact.
How to identify a health and safety or environmental issue and how to report it in accordance with organisational and legal procedures.
Ways of communicating with the public through social media to promote work.
Styles and methods of communicating with colleagues, volunteers and visitors using site interpretation, face to face interaction, electronic communication and telephone.
Safe manual handling practices, when personal protective equipment should be worn.
How to write risk assessments to mitigate risks to the public and themselves.
Practical first aid techniques for use in outdoor situations.

How to construct water control measures to slow the flow of water to reduce erosional impact from increased rainfall, for example water deflector, sluice or leaky dam, using local sustainable materials.
How to navigate in the countryside using a map and compass, satellite navigation and global positioning systems and how to use digital maps in a work context.

How severe and frequent weather events, including those caused by climate change, impact the countryside, including habitats, flora, fauna, and water levels, and how management practices can be adapted to lessen the impact at local and national levels.
Environmental best practice and the importance of using products from the local area or from sustainable sources.

How conservation work complements and interacts with other land management uses and interests, for example recreation, archaeology and historic environment, game keeping, tourism, estate management, farming or forestry.
How legislation governs the use and protection of the countryside and ecosystems, and how to apply this legislation in practice at both local and national levels.

The environmental impact of conservation work, including, the use of vehicles, fuel for machinery and dealing with waste materials, and how to sustainably improve working practices to benefit the environment.
Digital technology and digital systems and associated GDPR and cyber security policies and procedures.
Methods to identify and undertake learning and continuing professional development opportunities.

behaviours.detail: Positively embraces working outdoors in the countryside and environment, undertaking practical tasks in all weathers.
Demonstrates a strong work ethic including commitment, diligence and pride in doing a job well.
Works independently and in a team environment, building positive relationships with colleagues and volunteers.
Promotes safe working practices for themselves and others.
Respectful of others and promotes equity, diversity and inclusion with the team.
Considers the impact on the environment when using resources and carrying out work.

skills.detail: Construct or repair boundaries using different fencing types, for example post and rail, stock fencing or walls using local and natural materials.
Establish new hedging or manage existing hedging.
Construct or maintain suitably drained access ways using appropriate materials.

Identify British flora and fauna both native and non-native to the specific local area, for example, commonly seen birds, mammals, insects, herbs, flowers, trees or fungi, to determine the appropriate habitat management needed.
Carry out surveys which inform site management plans and work plans.
Use of brushcutters to clear vegetation.

Manage habitats and vegetation using specialist techniques and tools appropriate to the task and conservation requirements, dependant on seasonality, for example, strimming pathways, using pesticides, managing trees and hedgerows, and eradicating invasive species.
Use chainsaws for felling trees up 380mm and cross-cut timber.
Construct or maintain site furniture for access and interpretation, for example, bridges, gates, stiles, boardwalks, signs/waymarks or information boards using sustainable materials where possible.
Carry out pre use safety checks of hand tools and powered tools.
Produce and apply dynamic risk assessments.
Identify and apply safe working practices, including manual handling, and use personal protective equipment in accordance with manufacturer guidelines.
Apply practical first aid techniques in outdoor situations.
Construct water control measures to slow the flow of water to reduce erosional impact from increased rainfall, for example water deflector, sluice or leaky dam, using local sustainable materials.
Minimise negative environmental impact by following workplace policies when selecting and using vehicles, fuelling machinery and dealing with waste materials.
Use digital technology and systems in accordance with GDPR and cyber security policies and procedures.
Adapts behaviour and communication style to suit the audience for example colleagues, volunteers, visitors, landowners, other professionals and the public.

eQAProvider.contactAddress:
Ofqual
Earlsdon Park, 53-55 Butts Road, Coventry, CV1 3BH

involvedEmployers: Birmingham City Council, Cannington College, Chartered Institute of Ecology and Environmental Management (CIEEM), Kingston Maurward College, Lantra, National Trust, Natural England, North York Moors National Park, Redcar and Cleveland Council, RSPB, Sparsholt College, Yorkshire Dales Millennium Trust, Yorkshire Wildlife Trust
otherInvolvedStakeholders: Cannington College, Kingston Maurward College, Chartered Institute of Ecology and Environmental Management (CIEEM), Lantra, Sparsholt College.
englishAndMathsQualifications: <p>English and maths qualifications must be completed in line with the <a rel="noopener" href="https://www.gov.uk/guidance/apprenticeship-funding-rules" target="_blank">apprenticeship funding rules</a>.</p>
reviewDetails: this apprenticeship will be reviewed in accordance with our change request policy.
clusterDescription: Manage diverse environments, providing technical support in a range of specialist areas

qualifications.title: an award or training and assessment in first aid at work +F or forestry first aid +F or forestry first aid minimum 1 day course (face-to-face), carried out by either an awarding organisation, a UKAS accredited trade body or voluntary aid society recognised by government as specified in HSE document GEIS 3.
qualifications.anyAdditionalInformation: Please refer to the Awarding Organisation to confirm if English and maths is an essential component of any mandatory qualification.

qualifications.title: a level 2 qualification in chainsaw maintenance and cross cutting
qualifications.anyAdditionalInformation:
Lantra awards Level 2 Award In chainsaw Maintenance and Cross Cutting (600/5701/4) or City & Guilds NPTC Level 2 Award in Chainsaw Maintenance and Cross Cutting (600/6161/3). 
Please refer to the Awarding Organisation to confirm if English and maths is an essential component of any mandatory qualification.

qualifications.title: a level 2 qualification in felling
qualifications.anyAdditionalInformation:
Lantra awards Level 2 Awards in Felling and Processing trees up to 380mm (600/5703/8) or City and Guilds NPTC Level 2 Certificate of Competence in felling small trees up to 380mm (603/7354/4). 
Please refer to the Awarding Organisation to confirm if English and maths is an essential component of any mandatory qualification.

qualifications.title: a level 2 qualification in the safe use of brushcutters and trimmers qualification
qualifications.anyAdditionalInformation:
Lantra Awards Technical Award in Brushcutters/Trimmers –  (training with assessment) or City & Guilds NPTC Level 2 Award in the Safe Use of Brush-cutters and Trimmers (601/1299/2). 
Please refer to the Awarding Organisation to confirm if English and maths is an essential component of any mandatory qualification.

qualifications.title: a level 2 qualification in the use of pesticides
qualifications.anyAdditionalInformation:
Lantra Awards Level 2 Award in the Safe Application of Pesticide using Hand Held Equipment  (601/6562/5) (PA1 and PA6a combined)  Or Lantra awards Level 2 Award in the Safe Use of Pesticides (PA1) (601/5977/7) and Lantra Level 2 Safe Use of Pesticides and Hand Held Applicators (without safe use) (601/6562/5) (PA6a) or City & Guilds NPTC Level 2 Principles of Safe Handling and Application of Pesticides (PA1) and City & Guilds NPTC Level 2 Award in the Safe Application of Pesticides Using Pedestrian Hand Held Equipment (PA6a). 
Please refer to the Awarding Organisation to confirm if English and maths is an essential component of any mandatory qualification.

duties.dutyDetail: Build and repair field boundaries including fences, walls and hedging.
Maintain pathways or public access for walkers, cyclists and horse riders.
Use surveys to monitor flora, fauna and habitats and to assist in determining approaches to access management.
Identify and manage habitats and vegetation in accordance with management plans.
Select and perform maintenance on tools and equipment in accordance with manufacturer recommendations.
Liaise with the internal and external stakeholders using face to face interaction, electronic communication and telephone.
Work safely, following rules and regulations to protect self, colleagues and the public.
Keep up to date with advances in working practices and technologies.
Identify how to integrate environmental conservation with other land use interests and the legislation that applies.
